Hello ladies and gentlemen and welcome to the AWAI teleconference series. Today we're talking with the folks who are joining us at the business of Copywriting Academy with Dan Kennedy. Katie, take it away. Great. Well welcome everybody and we're looking very forward to seeing you next month. This is our second pre-academy prep call and basically we're going to be letting you ask as many questions of Dan as you like towards the end of this. So we're leaving plenty of time at the end for question and answers. To start Dan, I'd like to hear, I know you've had a chance to review the questionnaires that everybody's filled out and I just wanted to know what your observations are so far based on what you've seen. There's a pretty wide range of attendees speaking in terms of level of what's going on in their business and income. I mean there are full-time freelancers attending who are citing a quarter of a million, 300,000 a year and up, and there are aspiring attending who still have full-time jobs somewhere. In some cases they seem to like them, in other cases they seem not, who are earning little from copywriting and have marked themselves as aspiring and everything in between. So it's definitely affected my preparations. Okay, good. I mean it's causing me to make sure that there is balance for the very seasoned pros who are doing well without leaving anybody behind and plenty for the folks who are still trying to get their sea legs and everybody in between. It's pretty apparent that almost everybody is sort of coming around to the acceptance of the importance of marketing themselves, creating more than one source of business coming to them and really approaching this as a business. And it's apparent in their remarks on the questionnaires that this is not in most cases the mental place that they started, but it is where they are arriving. And so that's a good thing. There's great open-mindedness from all but a couple. There is one registered attendee who will either drive out of the room and do a refund too. I bet we win him over. I bet you win him over. Or we'll win him over. I think he is pretty hung up on the idea that success is related more to luck and bumbling into the right place at the right time. Well, I think it's pretty lucky he's decided to come. Yeah, that's good. It's somewhat odd. But he's nearly a minority of one. He else is really sort of on the right mental track, I think, for this to be a good experience for them. Probably the most interesting thing. It's not necessarily surprising, but it is interesting. I think it's that most, including those doing well, not just aspiring or those in the 50,000 to below six-figures range, but also on the questionnaires to what I would call psychological emotional angst over money, credentials, experience, self-confidence. And so they are underselling themselves out of their own attitudinal issues rather than marketplace realities. And they sort of know it, but don't know how to get past it. Again, they're going to be in the right place. Yeah, I'm going to talk a little bit more about that than I originally intended. But it is in other professions as well where ultimately somebody has to look face-to-face someone else in the eyes and quote a fee. So my experience with chiropractors, dentists, consultants, artists, pretty much anybody in that category, this range of emotional issues that get in their own way is universal. It's very commonly found. And it does dramatically affect somebody's ability to not only to get paid with their worth, but really to gain the confidence of a client, regardless of what they're charging them. And it's very, very apparent on the questionnaires. I've decided I'm not going to embarrass anybody by name. I don't think I will anyway, but I'm going to provide it on the questionnaires. But I am going to use some of what people have said and call on some people to talk about some of these issues because it's clearly important to just about everybody in the room. So I don't think I was really surprised by anything, but there's definitely been impact on time allocation to different things that we will talk about during the three, three days. That's great. And that's why those questionnaires are so important and useful tools. Yeah, many people indicated they were surprised at both how detailed they were and how challenging they were for them to fill out. Right. And whether done by questionnaire or done by interrogation, I always find that it's useful to probe the thinking, whether a one-on-one client or a group that you're going to work with, rather than sort of rushing into the prescription and delivery stage. Because people often, they go long periods of time without being very self-aware. And yet in many cases have a lot of the answers to their own issues and from a consulting standpoint can tell you what you need to turn around. And tell them to do. And tell them, yeah. Yeah. So, you know, this is a process I've gone through over the years. We're just about every multi-day seminar I've done, as well as one-on-one work. And I think probably a lot of people don't do it enough with their clients, particularly if they have been writing by assignment rather than really being involved at the strategy level. Right. And then they may not have been doing much of this with their own clients. And it's to the client's detriment and to theirs not to be doing it. I agree. And that is one of the many things I'm just so impressed with working on you on this project and other projects is how much homework and preparation and thought you put into things before you even, you know, get started. And it's very impressive. And I think it's going to be a powerful lesson for people to see. I prefer to know if it's Boxer's Briefs, Granny Panties or Thongs and what color, even if I have no idea why I want that information. Right. It's sort of the more you have, the more possibility there is to discover something useful. Right. That's great. Anything else on the questionnaires? No. I, we're closed in case anybody hasn't sent it in, don't bother. Yeah. Okay. Because I'm done with that stage of my preparation. Okay. Good. Now, what would you like the attendees to be thinking about and how would you like them to be preparing themselves between now and May 6? Well, many people mentioned, you know, that they have read or been reading the material that we sent them, which that will be helpful. You sent them the 48 Laws, I think. Yes. Which a couple of people have said has made them very uncomfortable. But. Oh, good. Yeah. That's right. That's absolutely right. Glad I sent it. But that it is, you know, provoking thought, which is important. So, as much of that reading as people can get done, you know, the better. Beyond that, I have a little list of 10 questions, you know, that they can be thinking about. And if it's okay, I'll just rattle through those. Sure. Sure. One is giving thought to who are you for? And by that, I mean, certainly in the early stages, just about everybody is sort of for anybody who will give them money. And when I started speaking, the sort of the inside joke about it and speaking, which is actually true, is the phone call comes into the new speaker's office. It's some meeting planner who's looking for somebody to speak at a convention on the sex lives of New Zealand frogs. And you are instantly an expert on the lives of New Zealand frogs, even if you weren't even aware they had frogs in New Zealand. And it's understandable that everybody sort of does that when the phone isn't ringing much and the phone bill is due. But the quicker you transition out of it, the better. And so a lot of thinking has to go into the client, who you are well matched with and are most likely to be successful with, based upon everything from temperament to experience, to interest, to skills. And essentially, the clearer a mental picture, as well as a statistical one, a business description one, the clearer the picture you have of the ideal client, the easier it is then to craft a business that can bring you those ideal clients. In the same vein, the second question is really who can support you as you wish to be supported? And the questionnaires reveal some variety in desired income, which is good. And so there's a difference between somebody for whom six figures is perfectly satisfactory and somebody for high six figures is the minimum and someone who likes their day job and wants to freelance part time. And the income is even a secondary issue. And 30,000, 40,000 a year extra is a good number. So the number matters because it sort of tells us who the right size and the right type of client is in terms of their opportunities. And so people really need to be thinking about what they want their income to look like. But then beyond that, what type of client, what size of client, what kinds of opportunities does the client need to have for you to leverage into the income that you want? Another couple of questions that link together are what is your specialized knowledge or skill and what are all the assets, the unique assets that you bring to the table? And this is a very broad question because a lot of people have assets that they don't know they have. So for example, as you know, I recently went through the exercise of picking a few AWI folks to assist on a project and about 100, I think 103 people submitted information. And some really only focused on what I would call resume assets. Here's who I've written for, here's the type of writing I've done, here's the awards I've won, here's where I've been published, here's the school I graduated from, here's the number of years I've been doing this, you know, that sort of thing. Others were pretty astute about talking about what I would call personal assets relevant to this project so that they're a mom, that they have teenage daughters, that they work with a youth group. A lot of things that are important if we are going to be creating advertising that appeals to teenagers or for that matter to moms of teenagers. So somebody's life experience, their current life circumstances, their gender, their ethnicity, their passions and interests, all of these things can be important assets and can constitute specialized knowledge or skill relevant to a particular type of client. A particular product category, service category, business category and so forth. Then there's also all the skill assets. I mean, are you particularly adept at converting leads to kept appointments in a showroom, store or office? Or are you particularly adept at getting people to show up at webinars? Or what are you particularly adept at? And so you really want to think about all, every possible asset that you have that may be of value to some clients, some clients in some product categories. Then sort of the next question is how can you play to your strengths in attracting the desired clients? So pretty much everybody would put writing down as a strength. So obviously there's ways to leverage writing to attract clients. But then there's issues of should you be very actively networking or not? For some people that's a strength. For other people like me, who are fundamentally anti-social, that's a weakness. Should you seek opportunities to speak as a means of attracting clients or not? Well, for some people that may be a strength as it is for me, although a developed one, not a natural one. For others that may be a profound weakness. And since there's lots of ways to attract clients, it's really not necessary to be struggling to conquer and overcome weaknesses to do it. So a really kind of good idea of personal strengths and weaknesses. Another issue is what type of client should you be seeking now? What type of client do you think you want to work with? A year from now, two years from now, three years from now, what about five, six, seven years from now? So we're going to talk about short-term, medium-term, and long-term strategy occurring simultaneously rather than sequentially. And you may very well have some types of clients that are both appropriate and accessible now that you know you're going to want nothing to do with five years from now. And so that's okay as long as you have a transition strategy. This also refers to the level of sophistication of the client. Some people prefer marketing ignorant clients. Some people prefer really, really sophisticated clients. Some people prefer B2B. Some people prefer consumer. Big company, small company. And there's reasons to choose them now. There's reasons to choose them as your business develops. And so that's something to be thinking about and something we'll be talking about. A good question to be thinking about is what targeted group of prospective clients have you been grooming? Have you been developing? Have you got in your pipeline? This month's Marketing Your Services newsletter is all about farming, which is a sales term for developing a group of prospects through frequent and continuous contact. Interesting. And again, my sense of the question airs, what are you doing to get business question? I think one of the things that I think is important is that very few people are either aware of or are using this strategy where they have a target group of clients that they are methodically planting seeds with and developing over a period of time, not necessarily expecting to go from cold phone call, cold solicitation to business instantly. Another good question is how can you increase your value to match the desired income and the type of client that you want? So it really is, what are you doing to make yourself more valuable? And some question airs, there's profound evidence of that. Other question airs, there's really a complete absence of it. What they're reading is not necessarily increasing their value. Who they're paying attention to is not necessarily increasing their value. So we're going to talk about that. And then we're going to talk about what the bridge between their present income and their stated income goal ought to look like. So everybody who turned in a question air, and incidentally pretty much everybody... Yeah, I think we're pretty close to 100%. Yeah. Everybody has an income goal and everybody has a current income number. And so there's a chasm between the two. For many of the people, it's a pretty big chasm. And so you need to be thinking about what does that bridge look like? What's going to be required in order to get from where you are to this number that you have cited in terms of numbers of clients, types of clients, amount of work, size of opportunity in the hands of the client, how you get paid. So we're really going to be building that bridge and people ought to be thinking about that bridge. And finally, what is the business providing that income goal look like? So what does this person see themselves two years from now? Most of the people cited a number two years out or three years out. What does that business really look like? Are the clients local, national, global? Are they in one niche? Are they diversified? Are you traveling to them? Are they coming to you? Is the work all being done by distance? Are you getting paid fees only? Or are you sharing in upsides? How are you getting that business? And that mental picture is pretty important. For most people, it tends to be very, very fuzzy. We want to get to great clarity because the clearer it is, typically the faster it's achieved. So those are ten things that are going to underlie the work we're going to do and ten things that folks can kind of be mulling over ahead of time. I love it. I mean, it's just setting this foundation for what's really going to be a transformative event. I could just see it. I don't know how it can't be. It's very exciting. Do you want to spend the rest of our time letting people play? Sure. Sure. Denise? Yes, Katie? Let's open it up and see if we have any questions. All right. Alicia, are you there? I am. Hi, Alicia. This is the operator. We've got callers on the line. Would you please explain to us how they go about asking a question? Sure. Ladies and gentlemen, if you would like to ask a question, please press star one on your keypad. You will then hear a message saying that you have entered the queue. If you are somehow removed from the queue, please press star one again. I will open the lines one at a time and you may ask your question. I will ask you to state your name first. When you hear me repeat your name back into the conference, you may ask your question. Again, that option, ladies and gentlemen, is star one. Alicia, if I can just interrupt for a minute because I think last time we did have some people that hit star one twice. If you do that, then you are out. You only hit star one once. You only need to do star one once. Okay. As of now we do have one question. Would you like to go ahead? Our next question is caller in queue. Would you state your name please? Donald. Donald, you may go ahead with your question. I'm just curious if the session is going to be recorded. DVDs. Is it Donald or Donald, which do you prefer? Donald. Okay. Donald, we are recording it, but we have no idea whether we'll ever release it. What I will tell you is if recordings become available of it, they will be edited highlights. When I do a multi-day session like this that I consider to be advanced and when I've collected a lot of information from participants in order to tailor it to them, I tend to be candid and forthright and not really editing myself as I speak. There's undoubtedly going to be things said in the room that I want you to have, but I don't necessarily want published for circulation to anybody and everybody across the planet. So the likelihood of complete recordings of the event ever being available is extremely slim. The likelihood of some highlights version of it is probable. If you're concerned about retention, if that's what the question is about, you will have a lot of reference materials and notes. And notes with you when you walk out the door. But also, a lot of this is not so much about what you might memorize, but about the thinking you'll do as we go as a result of being in the room. So it's as much about you leaving differently rather than you leaving with eight steps to this and four steps to that. I mean, you will leave with documents and notes and all that, but your need to have a complete recording of the three days to listen to 27 times is not that great. Very good. Alicia, do we have another question? Oh man, there are no questions at this time. Wow. Well, a shy group is going to make for a... There is one more question. Say there's always... See if you... Hello. Tyler, would you set your name please? Hello. Hi. This is Charles Morrissey. I've done what you said to do, star one. We got you. We're here. That's Dan. The question I have for you, Dan, is how did you go about selecting your very first client? You know, that's a very good question. And the answer is that the first clients were very much, really as I said at the beginning of the call, the criteria was not strict. It's like the criteria was anybody who would give you money. And so the first few clients are actually not even memorable to me. And we're really small time stuff. The first significant change in all that was when I changed from traditional advertising to direct... a response, maybe six months into doing work for clients. And then began to be selective based on the size and scope of their opportunity. Not so much their existing size as a company per se, but the size and scope of their opportunity. One of the earliest clients was a bowling ball manufacturer of really upscale bowling balls who was regional and who was doing their marketing by one method only. Consequently, if you think about that quickly in terms of opportunity, that says they're regional, they could be national. They're only using one method, one sales channel. There's four, five, six that they could be using. Another early client was a company with a fairly large sales force in the agricultural industry. And so they had distribution. They had needs for appointment. They had needs to pre-sell so that the sales people were more effective. The attraction of these kind of clients was, which I'll talk about at the Academy, was making myself visible to certain groups by more than one means. And attracting to me owners of businesses who knew they had opportunities that they weren't effectively exploiting. But to be absolutely fair, the first year probably was more about who would write a check than about anything else. But there was fairly quick evolution of, gee, there's people who could write a check for whom I'm a very poor fit or where there's unlikely to be a successful outcome. There's clients who it's a little more difficult to get them to write a check, but they're a much better fit or there's a much better opportunity. And so over about 12 months I got more and more sophisticated about that. And the most important thing that I figured out and made a point of getting good at was having more to choose from than I needed. So one of the things you see in any freelancing business is the person who gets a client, or typically they think in terms of gets a job, and then stops doing anything but doing the work on that job for that client until it's done. And then they start worrying about getting another client, which is very much like the caveman who went out and killed something, drug it back to the cave, skinned it, put it on a spit, and if it was big enough to feed them for a week, they didn't leave the cave for a week until the dinosaur was all eaten and then they went looking for another one. And the problem with that is, is occasionally there aren't any slow dinosaurs. So I figured out that it was best to have multiple pipelines filled with clients reaching maturity, meaning willingness and readiness to actually engage me and do a project, moving towards me through those pipelines all the time, so that most of the time I had more to choose from than I could handle rather than less. And that allows you to be discriminatory in who you accept. So the establishing of the groups of prospective clients who are moving towards you and reaching maturity at their own pace is really pretty important. And thanks for the question. And have we got anybody else who wants in? There are no more questions at this time. Again, ladies and gentlemen, if you would like to ask a question, please press star one. I would just caution everybody that the format of the Academy is not three days of lecture. So it works more like this. I'll present some material and then we'll talk about that material. And it will be up to you to participate. And if everybody sits there like stumps on a log, I'll take a break. I won't go back and talk some more. So this has to be a participatory process when you get here. You know from the material from which you enrolled what the main subject matter modules are. And so it will be good for you to give thought. As your questionnaires indicate that you have, it will be good for you to give thought to the questions you want to pose, the conversations you want to have, and to be doing the same as I'm presenting a hunk of material than thinking about the conversation that you want to have. Because the best value you can get here is if this is a conversation, not just a static presentation. And Dan, I can assure you that you're going to have your hands full based on some of the attendees that are on this list. There's quite a few of them coming who, like you say, some of them are clearly motivated and are having new success. But knowing some of these members, they're ready to engage you. I think it's going to be very... Well, that's good. Yeah. That's good. We do have two questions at this time. Okay, we'll take two more and then we'll wrap it up, I guess. Go ahead. Very good. Collin, would you say your name, please? Cindy Sear. Okay, go ahead with your question, Cindy. My question is, do you have suggestions for...if you have a client that you started with parameters and it kind of got out of control because it was a big client and it was a good opportunity and now they're really happy and they want you to do more work. But you've already...they're used to you doing stuff at a lesser rate, I guess, so now you want to bump your rate up. Do you have suggestions for how to handle that? Yeah, and there...well, when you say out of control, are you referring just to the fact that you... they got an enormous bargain and you now want to raise the fee or is it out of control in other ways, too? I would say that they got an enormous bargain. Okay, but otherwise they're well-behaved. They're well-behaved, yes. Okay, all right. Well, hey, it could be worse. Yeah. Yeah, because like I have a fairly new one right now who is cheating based upon our agreements process and like I'm getting the brain fart facts of the day and I have a feeling if I use email I'd be getting one every hour and so they have to be reeducated. So on my to-do list today, actually I'll be talking to my assistant after this call and I'm going to sickvicky on them to remind them of the rules of engagement. So it could be worse if you have a well-behaved client through whom you gave a great bargain. It could be worse. You know, it's interesting I had a client that I began doing work for 1981, I think, give or take. And I did a lot of work for them for a number of years and made them a lot of money. And as my compensation demands went up, I grandfathered them at first because they were also a great cash aid client. But ultimately, you know, had to have the conversation of guess what, you know, you're now paying about one-third of what everybody else is paying and that's not good. And I was able to inch them up some, but eventually they just, my fees had escalated to such a level that they just couldn't wrap their heads around paying that kind of money when it seemed like only yesterday to them that they had been paying so much less. And they basically went away and they were gone for almost a dozen years before returning, by which point, of course, my fees had escalated far beyond where they had been when they wouldn't pay them. And we got to the point of a discussion of a project and the fees and so forth and they gagged and the blood drained out of their face and their underwear got tight. And they went away for about a week and then they came back and I've been doing work for them ever since. I tell you that because as you attempt to deal with this, as good a client as they may be, if you aren't willing to lose them, if push comes to shove, then you are severely restricted in your ability to deal with this. Having said that, the advice is a combination of demonstration of value up until now and then gentle but frank conversation about the fact that they got a great bargain and that other clients are paying substantially more. And in order to do your best work for them, you can't continue to under price. Coupled with something to make them feel good. So still some sort of preferred client discount but yet they are paying more than they have been paying. Or some shift more perceived than real but some shift of a portion of the higher fee to performance based compensation if you haven't already been doing that. And or some value added that helps make up the difference. In my coaching business every year for five years I raised the fee and people started in that program. We were charged $9,000 a year and six years later we were charged $34,000 a year. And every time I did it I found a way to add some value that wasn't a great deal of skin off my nose so that it wasn't an apples to apples, you're just paying more for the same. And so a real question is how can you make it look like, make it feel like and to some extent make it that you are not just asking them to pay more for the same. So that the deliverable has changed at the same time the compensation has changed. Does that make sense to you? Are you still on the line? It makes sense to me. I think Cindy's probably stepped off the phone but that makes very good sense then. And you'll be impressed when she tells you who your client, her client is when she gets up there. Just happened it was pretty exciting. Okay we have time for one more call if that caller is still there. Okay. Caller in queue, set your name please. Caller, Leigh Ann. Please ask your question. Hi Diane, hi Dan. Hi there. Hi. This is sort of a related question and it's actually having to do with compensation structures. I know you talk in the literature about a combination of fees, commissions, referrals, percentages, things like that. How do you structure a compensation agreement and this may not be something you can answer on the phone other than at a high level so that you have enough control over the transactions that a commission agreement would work? Okay. Well I'll give you a partial answer. Okay. Some of it is contractual language and the actual architecture of the way these things work, which I'll take you through some of that at the academy. And it is too complicated for ten minutes on the phone. But there are two other issues that are not construct. One is selection. And so these arrangements working have a great deal to do with who's in the arrangement. And the very same structure can make for a very happy marriage with one party and a very ugly, ugly, ugly, unpleasant relationship to which everybody is thrilled to get out of with another. And so a great deal of this has to do with the type of the client, the mindset of the client. And all of the languageings that goes into the relationship from the very beginning. Because sharing in upside, if not managed well, can lead to resentment. And so I'm going to talk about this today, two days ago, with a client of mine who was here. He's based in Toronto. And amongst other things, he's paying a royalty on a campaign I created for him nine years ago. And I haven't touched since. And some people would begrudge that. And so he has in his case, not that he does it occasionally, in good humor, grouse about it. And not that occasionally I don't have to in good humor, remind him of how happy he ought to be. But generally speaking, he has the right attitude about it. And so clients, for example, are typically better suited to this than big, dumb corporations. And usually with big, dumb corporations, for example, you're going to have to have buyout clauses. And then you're going to have to have to have a big, dumb person. That's what I'm saying. And that's what I'm saying. And I'm not saying that you're going to have to have a big, dumb person. But that's what I'm saying. And that's what I'm saying. And the third thing I would say about it is, is that almost everybody is going to cheat a little. If not on purpose, at least accidentally. But in many cases, on purpose. And a little larceny is okay because it makes him feel better. A lot of larceny is not good at all. But room for a little larceny, I mean, I doubt that I'm getting every penny I'm supposed to get. But I'm getting enough pennies because they want to motivate me to work on the next thing, which I just described another thing that's important about the selection of the client, that their opportunities suggest that there's going to be a next thing and a next thing and a next thing. And so there are contractual clauses, for example, about inspecting books and records. And depending on what type of business they're in, how it gets tracked. But between you, me, and the fence post, in 30-some-odd years of this, I've never once invoked that clause. I've never once gone and inspected books and records. I'm quite sure I'm not getting every penny I'm supposed to get, but I'm getting plenty of pennies. And so this really is about three things. It's about the selection of the client, having the right client hooked to the right compensation structure. So different compensation structures are best for different clients. And secondly, having the right definition of the relationship, the right language, the right paperwork in place. And third, kind of being okay with knowing that some stuff is slipping through the cracks, but overall, you're doing well. So I hope that provides some insight. The most important thing probably is matching the right client to the right structure for the relationship. Thank you. I think I need to work on thing number one. Okay. All right. Good. Oh, great. All right, gang. I think we'll check out. Okay. Well, Dan, thank you so much. And we'll see you in just a few weeks. Okay. And if you could very quickly give me a call at my private number. Okay, sure. And everybody else, I'll see you in a few weeks. Thank you, Dan. Thanks, Katie.
